name: Tlaltecuhtli
description: Tlaltecuhtli, the Earth Goddess in Aztec mythology, was a monstrous and fearsome being. She was depicted as a squatting toad-like creature with a wide mouth full of teeth, often devouring the sun at dusk. Tlaltecuhtli was the source of both life and death, embodying the duality of the earth. Her body was said to be fragmented during the creation of the world by Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca. These gods tore her apart, with her top half becoming the sky and her lower half becoming the earth. Because of this violent act, Tlaltecuhtli constantly demanded human sacrifices to appease her insatiable hunger and ensure the continued fertility of the land. She controlled earthquakes, brought forth vegetation, and was the ultimate source of sustenance. She was a powerful, ambivalent figure, both nurturing and terrifying, representing the raw power and unpredictable nature of the earth itself. Her association with childbirth linked her to the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. She was also connected to the underworld and the spirits of the dead. As a primordial being, Tlaltecuhtli represents the untamed and primal forces that shaped the Aztec cosmos. She embodies the constant struggle between order and chaos, and the necessity of sacrifice to maintain balance. She is often depicted with prominent breasts, representing her nurturing aspect, and skeletal features, symbolizing her connection to death and decay. Despite being a fearsome deity, she was also revered as a provider and sustainer of life.
subtitle: Earth Goddess of Life and Death

keyMyths.title: Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca tore Tlaltecuhtli apart to create the sky and the earth, leading to her constant demand for human sacrifice. This act established the cosmic order and the need for appeasement.

keyMyths.title: Tlaltecuhtli's Hunger
keyMyths.description: Tlaltecuhtli's insatiable hunger requires constant offerings, especially human hearts and blood, to maintain the balance of the world and ensure continued fertility. Without these sacrifices, the earth would become barren.

keyMyths.title: Association with Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Tlaltecuhtli's connection to childbirth reflects the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. Women who died in childbirth were believed to have become companions of Tlaltecuhtli, highlighting the goddess's role in the transition between worlds.
